steps: Emergency? Call Public Safety 212-854-5555 first, then your RD. Otherwise notify your RD or post in the Health Chat that a student needs care.

Decide where: John Jay Health Center for mild issues during business hours (Mon–Thu 9am–5pm, Fri 9am–4pm, John Jay Hall — call to reserve before walking in); St. Luke's ER (113th & Amsterdam) for after-hours, weekends, medium/high, or a clear emergency.

If calling ahead, do it with the student present — explain who you are, then hand the phone to the student to describe symptoms. During business hours the verified Health Services line is 212-854-2284; the 24-hr CU Health line (212-854-7426) is unverified — confirm it on the SRA Master Contact Sheet. Notify your RD and file a Maxient report for any health incident.

Prevent it: promote water-drinking, take frequent breaks in shade/AC during activities, and be strict — if needed, stop the activity and make students (and yourself) hydrate. Avoid strenuous outdoor exercise in the heat; do it early morning or evening.
Notify your RD and file a Maxient report for any health incident.

title: A student discloses abuse to you — how to respond in the moment
tags: disclose disclosure discloses disclosing abuse abused minor child what do I say what to say keep a secret keep secret tell me a secret can't promise can't keep secret confidentiality confidential private quiet calm rapport open ended open-ended questions who what where not an investigator don't judge don't promise thank them excursion mandated reporter listening listen something bad at home someone hurt me someone hurt them kid wants to tell me something resident disclosed told me
teaser: Find a private, quiet space, stay calm, and just LISTEN. You can't promise to keep it secret. Get only who/what/where — then report (see the abuse-of-a-minor card).
steps: Move somewhere private and quiet — not the common room. Address their immediate needs first (bathroom, food, water).
Have two staff present if you can, but only ONE person talks — the other listens/takes notes. Sit at their eye level, not too close, not far.
Don't jump straight to the topic — build a little rapport first (“how's the program going?”); it lets you both breathe and helps them trust you.
Stay calm — this is critical. If you react badly or freak out, kids shut down and recant, which makes it much harder to get them help.
Let them talk. Ask simple, open-ended questions — “what happened?” not “who hit you?” (a leading question assumes the answer). Keep an open mind in case there's an innocent explanation.
You only need the basics: who, what, and where (“when” matters least). You are NOT an investigator — don't dig for the full story or graphic details.

NEVER promise secrecy. If they ask, the answer is “I can't promise that” — you're required by law and policy to report. Acknowledge their fear honestly; reassure them only the people who need to know will know.
Don't express judgment of the abuser or disbelief — the student may love the person, and voicing “that's terrible” can shut them down.
Don't make promises about what happens next (“everything will be okay”) — you don't know. Take your time.
Thank them for telling you. Then follow your reporting obligations (see “Suspected abuse or maltreatment of a minor”) and loop in the Wellness Coordinator for support.
On an excursion: don't interview them in the group — hang back, check they're emotionally okay and safe right now, and ask to follow up once back on campus.
note: This card covers the disclosure conversation itself — what to say in the moment. For WHO to call (911 / Public Safety / NY State Hotline 800-342-3720 / Protection of Minors form / Director), see the “Suspected abuse or maltreatment of a minor” card. Source: 6/15 Protection of Minors training (Rachel Ferrari).

title: When to call Public Safety / NYPD
tags: public safety nypd crisis call when threshold danger help unsure emergency alerts alert notifications text sign up signup is calling public safety the same as police does it mean the police are coming nypd ice immigration customs border patrol separate entities not police why call public safety instead of 911 on campus first internal ambulance does not charge work with immigration share data partnered international student
teaser: Call 212-854-5555 if a situation is nearing crisis. When unsure, err on the side of calling. Public Safety is NOT NYPD or ICE.
steps: Call Public Safety 212-854-5555 when behavior poses imminent danger of: harming self or others; impeding others' lawful activities; threatening health/safety/wellbeing; a health emergency; or online posts suggesting suicidal ideation or intent to harm.

Situations that usually warrant a call: violent/suicidal/homicidal behavior, seizures, labored breathing, physical injury, unconsciousness, hallucinations or intense pain, inability to speak/walk, or dangerous intoxication.
If you're unsure whether a crisis exists, err on the side of caution and call.

Calling Public Safety is NOT calling the police or immigration. Columbia Public Safety, NYPD, and CBP/ICE are completely separate entities — Public Safety has never partnered or shared data with those agencies (a key reassurance for international students).

On campus, call Public Safety FIRST (not 911): they have full oversight of campus, know the buildings and the high number of minors, and have their own internal ambulance that does not charge students. NYPD only comes onto campus in a true emergency, so they lack that consistent building access and familiarity.
Off campus, a medical emergency = call 911 first, then call Public Safety as a second call for their awareness and coordination.
Make sure you're signed up for Columbia Emergency Alerts so you get real-time safety notifications.

teaser: First confirm they're actually one of our residential students, bring them back within the boundaries, get their UNI, tell them they broke policy, and write a Maxient report. Don't chase anyone.
steps: Triple-check they're one of our residential students first. Commuter and other SPS students wear the same lanyard, so ask: are you a Pre-College / Columbia student? Get their UNI (usually 6–7 characters) and confirm against the residential roster.
Bring them back within the boundaries. You don't have to walk them all the way back to campus or their room — just back inside the boundary zone.
Let them know they're not allowed to be out of bounds, that they broke policy, and that you'll be writing them up and they'll receive a contact about it.
Write a Maxient report noting the student was found beyond the boundaries and that you returned them.

Do NOT chase a student. If they refuse to give their UNI or run off, don't pursue — most minors comply, and they have nowhere to go but back to campus. If you're on a trip when you spot an out-of-bounds student: same steps — confirm residential, get UNI, check the excursions list to see who's out, and reach out appropriately. (A commuter student heading home on their own is allowed — confirm first.)

title: Incident severity & who responds
tags: incident severity level minor medium major classification escalation who responds matrix low high tier notetaker chain how serious how bad bad enough big deal when to escalate when to call rd worth reporting serious enough wake the rd judgment call
teaser: Minor / Medium / Major — the bigger it is, the higher it escalates. RA is always on site; RD, Director, then Senior Leadership loop in by severity.
steps: Minor (roommate conflict, curfew, minor policy/health, ongoing depression/anxiety): the RA addresses it, notifies the RD (on campus) or Director (off campus), and files a Maxient report.
Medium (urgent non-emergency medical, hospital visit, escalated conflict, disruptive behavior, serious policy violation, theft, missing under 60 min): RA + RD on site; the Director is informed.

Major (hospitalization, urgent mental health, assault, drugs/alcohol, building/campus emergency, missing over 60 min, student death, Protection of Minors): RA + RD + Director on site; Senior Leadership informed.

For a major incident, the rhythm is: stay with the student, make your calls (Public Safety on campus / 911 off campus, then RD/Director), then become the notetaker once your supervisor arrives, and file a Maxient report.
details: Who responds, by severity Low: RA / Senior RA on site · RD / Coordinator informed Medium: RA + RD / Coordinator on site · Director informed High: RA + RD + Director on site · Senior Leadership informed

steps: If uncomfortable, just move — you don't have to talk to or stay near anyone. If someone is talking to you, say "Sorry. I have to go." and leave. Don't engage strangers; avoid staring at or photographing anyone behaving strangely.

If you feel unsafe or lost: approach a police officer, call your SRA, or step into a store. Near Columbia, look for Columbia Safe Haven stores — identified by a red-lion 'Columbia Safe Haven' decal in the window — that provide a secure place for students.
Getting back to campus: take the 1 train (stops right outside Columbia). Do NOT ride the 2/3 past 96th St (transfer to the 1 at 96th); the A/C to 110/116 are far from campus.

If something happens on a train/bus: report to an MTA employee or police officer before leaving the station — do not leave without making a report. Stay on scene for the report with all witnesses present.
Call the Director on duty immediately — don't wait until returning to campus — and file a Maxient report for every off-campus incident, big or small.

teaser: Off-campus, your first call is the Director on Duty: 'we're working to find a student.' Keep everyone else safe — a second RA can take the remaining students back (cab gets reimbursed) while you search.
steps: Call the Director on Duty FIRST. Because you're off campus / out of bounds, that's your point of contact (not Public Safety). Say you're working to find a student so they're aware in case a parent calls Columbia.

Use the trip's group chat to reach the student and their trip-mates ("does anyone know where so-and-so is?"). Set up a group chat before every trip — a Google Chat using LionMail emails avoids exchanging personal phone numbers.

Don't delay everyone else. If there's more than one RA, the other RA can take the remaining students back to campus by train or cab (cabs get reimbursed) while you and the Director on Duty work to locate the missing student.

If you find a separated student from another group: keep them with you, notify everyone appropriately, and call the Director on Duty for the best plan. Don't send a lost minor back alone unless the Director on Duty approves.

Prevention: get every rider's info before leaving, do periodic check-ins, and keep watching the IBC excursions chat while you're out — if Student Services is looking for a student, they post the name + UNI there.

title: Confronting a policy violation (step-by-step)
tags: confront policy violation enforce enforcement rule breaking caught student misbehaving address stop behavior id uni document witnessed de-escalate disruptive delegate crowd control other students away keep them away gossip privacy not everyone present handle alone with another ra second staff member don't promise outcome can't determine result punishment consequence guarantee tell them what punishment they'll get nothing bad will happen conduct coordinator avoid accusations only what i witnessed ask a peer then sra
teaser: Stay calm, identify yourself, ask them to stop, get their ID/UNI, explain the violation, say you're documenting it, set expectations, file an IR.
steps: Identify the issue, take a breath, then approach and introduce yourself as a staff member (give your name).
Ask the student to stop the behavior immediately, then ask for their ID card and UNI.
Tell them what you observed, ask for clarification, and explain why it violated a policy.
Let them know you're documenting it and filing an Incident Report, and set expectations for the rest of the night.
File the Incident Report (Maxient). Gather the CUIDs of all students involved (regardless of participation), photos, and the names of responding staff/officers; use your phone to note times.

Divide the roles. With two staff present, have one host the conversation with the student while the other keeps the rest of the residents away from the incident and any gossip. Not everyone needs to be present — the student wouldn't want the whole floor knowing their business.

Never promise a specific outcome. Speak only to what you actually witnessed (avoid accusations / assuming what they have going on). The result goes through the proper channels — even a Student Conduct Coordinator can't determine the outcome, so don't anticipate or guarantee it. If you're unsure how to proceed, ask a peer, then your SRA, then the Student Conduct Coordinator.

Do: stay calm, be firm and clear, take notes, de-escalate, get help if needed. Don't: match aggression, involve too many people, promise an outcome, reframe it as “okay,” or ever physically touch a student.

teaser: Usually notify parents on attendance, wellness, and behavior issues — but the student's safety at home comes first; loop in admin before notifying. The after-hours parent line is on the Master Contact Sheet (not posted here) — redirect parents who contact you to it or Student Services.
steps: In most cases staff communicate attendance, health, and behavior issues to a parent/guardian — but always weigh the student's safety at home and the right timing and method.

Some situations where safety takes precedence over notifying parents: consensual student–student sexual contact (if telling home could endanger the student), and Protection of Minors (a child reporting abuse at home).
For abuse-at-home / POM: contact the Director of Student Affairs (Programs for Minors) before deciding whether to call parents — there may be a continued threat at home.
Parental-notification calls aren't made alone — when unsure, consult your RD/Director.

If a parent contacts YOU directly: parents shouldn't be reaching out to you — there's a dedicated parent-concern line that the coordinators manage. Direct them to call the program / the Student Services Office; don't try to field the concern yourself.

The after-hours parent line (the number parents should call outside business hours) is listed on the Master Contact Sheet — it is not posted in this hub. Get the current number from the sheet or your SRA/RD, and direct parents there. Never hand out your own number.

Never share your personal contact info with parents or students. Do not put your phone number, email, or any contact info on your door decs or anywhere on your door — parents will take it down and contact you all summer. Don't give out other staff members' personal numbers either.

title: Fire alarm / building evacuation
tags: fire alarm fire drill the fire alarm went off alarm going off building evacuation evacuate evacuated evacuating get out building fire there's a fire smoke smoke alarm fire in the building stairs staircase stairwell stairwells never elevator don't take the elevator do not use the elevator go down not up head count count students assembly point assembly area designated lawn outside meet up rally point gather outside back of the hand hot door check the door wake students asleep last to leave the floor injured student can't get down stuck on the floor can't evacuate missing student after evacuation can't find a student fight the fire fire extinguisher fire hose hoses firefighter look for a uniform fire safety fire department fire life safety false alarm is it a drill real alarm
teaser: Treat EVERY alarm as real. Get students out via the stairs (never elevators), do a head count, and assemble at the designated outdoor area. Don't fight the fire — evacuate.
steps: Treat every alarm as real. Unless Public Safety or Facilities tells you it's a drill or false alarm, evacuate — "every alarm is a real alarm."
Get students out fast. You're responsible for your floor — go down the hall and hit every room, and wake anyone who's asleep. Be the last to leave the floor.
Use the staircases, NEVER the elevators (Public Safety recalls and controls them). Always go DOWN, never up. Know your two stairwells in advance.
If there's smoke, stay low (smoke and heat rise). Before opening any door, check it with the back of your hand, starting from the top — if it's hot, don't open it.
Do head counts. Count students before leaving the floor, then count again once you're outside. If the numbers don't match, tell Fire Safety / Public Safety immediately.
Assemble at the designated outdoor assembly point and keep clear of responding staff and the fire department.

If a student is injured and can't get down: get them onto a stairwell landing (out of the evacuation path), have one RA stay with them, and send a second RA down to tell Public Safety / Fire Safety exactly which floor and stairwell.

If a student is missing after evacuation: once everyone's at the assembly point and you still can't find a resident you believe is inside, notify on-site staff immediately — this triggers an emergency roll / missing-student response.
Don't play firefighter. Don't touch the wall fire hoses. Only consider a portable extinguisher for a tiny, contained fire with two people and your exit behind you — otherwise just get out.
To find Fire Safety / Public Safety in the chaos, look for a uniform — they can radio the fire department about anyone injured or left behind.
note: New — big safety gap; no existing card covered fire alarms/evacuation (closest are Active shooter and Student is missing). Source: 6/15 AM Fire Life Safety training recording. Public Safety number 212-854-5555 reused from existing verified cards; NO phone number taken from the recording. teaser: Commuters are day students: weekdays only, off campus in the evening, and NEVER inside the residence halls (they can't come in to visit a resident friend). They must live with adult supervision — if one tells you they're staying somewhere alone, flag it to admin right away.
steps: Commuter students are on campus weekdays only and must leave campus in the evening — they should not attend evening programming or weekend experiences. (A few approved evening events exist only with parent/guardian permission, coordinated by the office.)
They may access spaces like Butler Library, Lerner Hall, their classroom, and green/social space — but NEVER the residence halls. A commuter cannot come in to visit a resident friend.

While they're on campus, commuters are our responsibility: a commuter medical (or other) emergency = respond exactly as you would for a residential student (same protocol and immediacy), then admin coordinates next steps.

Commuters must live at a residence with adult supervision — with a parent/guardian, or a pre-arranged nearby family/older-sibling arrangement. They may not be unsupervised (no living alone in a hotel or Airbnb).

These rules apply even to commuters who are 18+: being of legal age does not exempt a student from the program's stipulations; they still need a guardian arrangement.

teaser: After in-room curfew, students must be in their OWN room. Before in-room they can visit another student's room only with everyone's permission and the door propped open. Students may only be on their own residential floor — wrong floor = a write-up.
steps: Floors are restricted: students may only be on their own residential floor. Hanging out happens in lounges / common areas (which is why those get staffed). A student actively on the wrong floor — or one you don't recognize — is a write-up: have the conversation and document it.

Visiting rooms: students can be in each other's rooms only BEFORE in-room curfew, and only with full permission from everyone who lives there (in a double, both roommates must agree — they sort this out in the roommate agreement at opening).

teaser: RAs use the STAFF version of the Orah app to run curfew roll checks (present / absent / excused / late), confirm out-of-bounds passes, and verify student locations. RDs see live who's still unchecked — watch the chat.
steps: Use the STAFF version of Orah (there are separate parent, student, and staff versions in the app store — the staff one shows ALL students). You can also use it in a browser at app.orah.com.

At each curfew the RD starts a roll call ~10–15 min early (e.g. ~8:45–8:50 for a 9pm in-building check). You'll get a notification + a note in the chat — open the roll, find your building, then your floor.
You're responsible for checking in only YOUR own assigned group (usually ~4–7) — not the whole floor. Exception: if a floormate is on their night off, you may cover their check-ins.

Lay eyes on each of your students and mark them present / absent / excused absence / on an approved program or pass. Identify students by ROOM number, not just name — students often share the exact same first/last name, and some go by a different name than their legal name in the app.

RDs and SRAs see live how many are still unchecked; the roll isn't closed until everyone is accounted for. If an RD says they're still missing people, go back and click in whoever's outstanding (don't be surprised by a call that none of your kids are checked in).

A student missing from the roll? Check their profile for an active or scheduled out-of-bounds pass first — students on an approved pass may not appear (a banner shows when a pass is active). Office staff schedule passes only after a parent submits the out-of-bounds form and they confirm by phone; students cannot add their own passes. If there's no pass, it may be a troubleshooting issue — flag it to your SRA / a returner.
Running late from a program and can't check in on time? Post it in the chat and ask a floormate to check your students (see the late-from-program / shift-cover guidance).

steps: Lobby table: staff your assigned station — greet entry/exit, enforce in-building curfew, help with building access and lockouts, escort Facilities, keep lounges tidy, manage elevator loads, update bulletin boards, and answer policy questions.
Lobby rules: keep your phone on (lockout / health-form calls), no napping, video games, or leaving the building except on official breaks, and dress appropriately (no pajamas).

Floater shift (Morning 9am–1pm / Afternoon 1–5pm): when you're not on a task, wait in the Student Services Office with your phone on. Tasks include student escorts, food pickup, errands, and program assistance.

title: RA–student boundaries (money, personal questions, social media, “keep it secret”)
tags: boundaries boundary money lend cash gift personal questions dating crush social media instagram follow friend hang out contact number professional near peer friendly not friends confidential secret don't tell mandated reporter relationship favoritism back up a colleague cornering pressuring another ra crush hitting on co-worker support female ras persistent asking for number watch out report intimidating teenager pestering
teaser: “Friendly, but not friends.” Never lend money or give gifts; don't share your dating life or socialize/DM students; redirect crushes kindly but firmly; you can't promise secrecy.
steps: Your stance is “friendly, but not friends.” You're a guide, resource, role model, and first-responder — not their peer, therapist, parent, or social-media buddy. There's a real power imbalance, so keep it professional.

Money: never lend money or pay for a student's meals/items, and don't give cash gifts. Instead help them solve it (grab their wallet, a friend spots them) or refer to campus resources / contact admins about meal-plan issues.

Personal questions / a crush: don't answer questions about your dating life — redirect. No exchanging personal contact info, socializing outside the program, adding students on personal social media, or any romantic/sexual relationship. Address it kindly but firmly and document concerning interactions.

Try: “I appreciate that you feel comfortable with me, but I'm here as your RA and mentor — I need to keep this professional. I won't be exchanging personal contact info or hanging out outside the program.”

Back up your colleagues: boundaries are a team thing. If you see a student persistently cornering or pressuring a fellow RA — e.g. a tall teenager pushing for their number — step in and don't leave them to handle it alone. Male RAs especially: be mindful and back up your female colleagues, since even with authority an intimidating situation can feel unsafe. Note what you saw, watch out for it, and flag concerning interactions to your SRA/RD early.

“Don't tell anyone” / confidentiality: you're a mandated reporter — never promise secrecy you can't keep; say so upfront. You must report suspected abuse/neglect, self-harm or suicidal ideation, illegal drug use or distribution, sexual assault/harassment, and any criminal activity. Student safety always beats confidentiality.
When unsure, lean on program policy, avoid making exceptions, and involve your supervisor. File a Maxient report for anything concerning.

teaser: Thank them, stay calm, and don't make it a bigger deal than they do. Ask whether the name/pronouns are for everyone or just you, never out them, and loop in your senior/Wellness if they need more than you can give.
steps: Respond simply and warmly — “Thank you for sharing.” Don't pull them aside for a Big Moment if they're treating it casually; that can make it awkward.

If they want a new name or pronouns, treat it as a conversation: ask whether to use it with everyone (floor, other RAs, instructor) or keep it just between you. Follow their lead on how “out” they want to be.

Never out a student. Use their chosen name/pronouns; if a parent uses a different name, fall back to neutral language (“your child / your student”) rather than the wrong name, and avoid contradicting in the moment.
Have resources ready if asked (gender-inclusive bathroom map, Columbia's queer/trans student resources) — but don't dump a packet on them unprompted.

You're not a therapist. If the conversation gets heavy or they need more than you can offer, say so kindly and connect them to more help — loop in your SRA/RD, the program, and the Wellness Coordinator.

teaser: Address within 24h. Prep, update supervisor, mediate, write an IR, follow up. Don't mediate physical/bias/Title IX.
steps: Prioritize mediations and address conflict within 24 hours.
Loop in your SRA/RD before you run it — by this point they should already at least be informed.

Prep: gather info, then meet each student independently first to hear their side before bringing them together; narrow to immediate parties, reserve a space (e.g. floor lounge) — notify other floors before booking a shared space — and set a time limit (≤1 hr; longer only if truly needed).
During: open with intro + ground rules, give equal time, let residents brainstorm solutions, review/edit the Roommate Agreement, plan follow-up.
After: update your supervisor on the outcome, write an Incident Report, and check in on the students within 24–48 hours (feelings often shift after a night's sleep).
Do NOT mediate physical altercations, bias/harassment, or Title IX incidents — escalate those.

teaser: Room switches are very hard to get and won't happen just because a student dislikes their roommate — push them to work it out first. Real moves need a clear, deliberate reason (e.g. a flooding room or a safety issue) and are decided up the chain (SRA → RD → conduct staff), not by you.
steps: Set expectations: a switch over a disagreement (cleanliness, bedtimes, tidiness) will almost never be approved — the program is packed and there's little room to move people.
First, push the students to resolve it themselves; run a roommate-agreement mediation if needed (see the mediation card).

steps: Watch for: loss of appetite, skipping social events or class, missing home/food/family, withdrawing, or talk of going home.

details: Common challenges & how to help Culture shock / homesickness: normalize their feelings, share resources, keep checking in. Academic adjustment: US classes expect participation/discussion, and what counts as “plagiarism/cheating” differs by country. Encourage office hours and asking instructors what's allowed. Communication: go easy on slang/idioms (sports idioms especially), check for understanding, don't correct their English, and watch for inside jokes in groups. Living in NYC: help with subway/MetroCard, tipping & sales tax, and where to shop. If they're anxious about subway/city safety, listen without judgement, give context vs. sensational news, and share street-smart tips. Visa / travel / belonging: be welcoming, celebrate differences without “othering,” redirect hurtful jokes, and thank students when they're vulnerable enough to share.

steps: Maintain a 1 RA to 5 residents ratio on every trip. The 5:1 ratio only applies once you leave the building — in-building programs don't need a second RA regardless of headcount (e.g. 10 students on one floor for a floor program is fine with one RA).

Weekly cadence (transcript-only — verify): at least 1 in-building program per week (aim for 2 in the FIRST week to build community); in-building programming runs about 4 hrs/week. Plus aim for 1–2 local excursions per week (~4 hrs each).
Lead cap (transcript-only — verify): a maximum of 3 excursions per week as the lead. Your SRA/RD can approve you to support additional excursions if it fits within your weekly hours.

NYC Uncovered is one of the required programming categories — but you do not need a single dedicated trip for it. Your normal trips throughout the week can each qualify, and at least 2 excursions/week should meet the NYC Uncovered criteria. (Attendance is optional but highly encouraged for students.) Lower confidence — confirm the exact NYC Uncovered requirement with your RD.
Submit your building's Program Proposal Form (Carman / Wallach + Lower John Jay / Upper John Jay) with a budget, and get RD approval before spending. Uphold the approved budget.

Timing (verify): submit the proposal no later than 3 business days before the program; reviewers have up to 2 business days to respond, so submit early. Don't buy anything until you get the email decision (approved / pending changes / denied).

Approval tiers (transcript-only — confirm): your SRA can approve an excursion if it's under ~$75 per staff member, within ~3 miles of campus, and fewer than 10 students. Anything over that — higher cost, farther, very crowded, or 10+ students — goes to your RD.

Budget caps (TRANSCRIPT-ONLY — verify with finance): up to ~$150 per in-building program (per program, not per staff); up to ~$125 per staff member on an excursion; ~$500 total per staff member for the session. Track spend through your SRA.
Students pay their own ticket/admission costs — keep their cost to roughly $100–$125 max, and mix in free programs so anyone can attend.
After the program: keep attendance records, complete the evaluation form, submit receipts digitally, and remove physical flyers once the event has passed.
For supplies, check the building RA office first (paint, canvases, leftover supplies); in-person with the P-card is often faster than ordering online.
Check the Programming Parameters & Requirements and the Approved Excursions List when planning.
Emphasize in-building programming for residents who can't afford/attend excursions.
Brief students on safety before leaving (charged phone, buddy system, MetroCard in advance, meet-up spot, head counts).

Off-campus incident: assess, call 911 if an emergency, report to MTA/police before leaving the station, stay for the report, call the Director on duty immediately, and file a Maxient for any off-campus incident.

steps: No R-rated movies — you're supervising minors.

No activities that require a waiver or contract. If students would have to sign a waiver, or staff would have to sign a contract on students' behalf (e.g. cliff jumping, many trampoline parks / boat rides), it needs legal sign-off — so it's generally a no.

teaser: Excursions and events post to the Engage app (the Columbia SPS campus-groups platform — heard in training as 'CS/SDS Engage'). Students browse a calendar and sign up there; RAs get 'officer access' to create and manage their own events. Always set the price to $0.
steps: Find the app: it's the Columbia SPS campus-groups platform (in training it was called 'CS/SDS Engage' — auto-captions vary, see note). Look for Campus Groups in the App Store / Play Store, or bookmark it in a browser. Log in with your normal Columbia login. You'll be added as an officer under the Pre-College group.

Post an event/trip: add a title + description, details (what to bring, dress code, food provided), tags (e.g. 'excursions'), a start/end date, and a meeting location — pasting a specific address auto-generates a map.
List YOURSELF as the event coordinator (not the admin), so student questions about your event come to you.

Set a registration close date a day or two before the event so you can confirm who's coming. You can save drafts and pull a posted event back to draft to fix mistakes.
Sharing: the editor can generate a share link or QR code for a flyer (no template exists — make your own).
If a student can't see or register, it's usually an access issue — email the programming admin, who can check the student's profile. Your SRA can also help edit events.

Then file the right form: Policy violation OR student of concern → Incident Report (Maxient) / bit.ly/spsconcern. All Maxient reports before 9am next day. Gender-based misconduct (harassment, assault, stalking, discrimination) → bit.ly/gbmreport Suspected abuse/maltreatment of a minor → SPS Protection of Minors report (+ 911 if danger, + NY hotline 800-342-3720, + Public Safety) Writing tip: focus on observable behaviors, give specifics (dates/counts), and avoid labels like “acting crazy.”

title: How to write an incident report (IR)
tags: incident report write writing ir maxient narrative how to fill out form objective facts third person past tense involved parties roles respondent complainant witness student of concern hall room documentation proofread lionmail
teaser: Third person, past tense, facts only (no opinions or slang). Gather date/time, location, full names+UNI, ID photos, behavior, calls. Always include Hall + Room.
steps: Before you write, gather: date/time, location, how you discovered it, full names + UNI (with roles), student ID-card photos, the behavior, who supported, calls/arrivals, and the end result.

Write: third person, past tense, facts only. Use a person's title + full name the first time, then title + last name after. Be specific enough that someone other than a Director can follow it. Proofread.

Avoid: first person, present tense, nicknames, vague umbrella terms, opinions, and slang (“weed,” “booze,” “sus,” “guilty,” “suspect,” etc.). Quote a student only if you wrote the quote down verbatim.
Facts, not labels: not “Doe was drunk” → “Doe had bloodshot eyes, an odor of alcohol, and was swaying.” Not “was rude” → quote what they actually said.

On the form: Reporter info = your full name, title, and LionMail. Under Involved Parties set each person's role (Student of Concern = wellness/FYI, no policy break; Respondent/Alleged = broke policy; Complainant/Victim; Witness; Staff Member Present) and always include Hall + Room (critical for follow-up).

Answer the prompts: “addressed with the student?” and “student aware a report is being written?” should be yes (tell them). “Immediate safety concern?” — if yes, call up. Attach supporting docs (at minimum the ID photos).

title: What happens after I file a conduct report (sanctions)
tags: conduct process sanctions hearing discipline probation gating gated what is gating what does gated mean sanction repeat violation late to curfew check in early early check-in no trips no excursions can't go on trips administrator notifies ra orah profile status sanction status dismissal no contact directive ncd warning certification outcome what happens after report kwesi
teaser: Report → routed to the Assistant Director → possible hearing → determination → sanctions. All violations include parental notification.
steps: After you file the Incident Report it's routed to the Assistant Director, who with a Coordinator decides whether the discipline process applies.

If it does: the student gets an email (allegation + hearing), two Pre-College staff (usually AD + 1) hold a hearing, then a determination is made — the student is presumed not responsible until then — and sanctions follow if responsible.

Sanctions can include: written warning, behavioral probation, campus gating (altered curfew / off-campus restrictions / tighter boundaries), a No Contact Directive, dismissal (depart within 48 hrs), and/or no Certificate of Participation. All violations include parental notification.

What is “gating”? It's a sanction for repeated smaller violations (e.g. a student who's repeatedly late to curfew) — a step short of dismissal. A gated student typically either must check in early for their curfew time, or keeps their normal curfew time but is not allowed to go on trips / excursions for a set period. If a student is gated it won't surprise you — the administrator who places the sanction reaches out to the affected RA to say it's been placed and for how long. You can see a student's gating/sanction status on the side of their profile in the Orah staff app.

Follow-up: wellness concerns go to the wellness team; conduct concerns to an RD/Admin; FYI reports are filed. Questions about an outcome → reach out to Kwesi Alleyne. Need to debrief after an incident? Reach out — that's encouraged.
